{"text":"Shinde, solar vr evda focus nai karayla pahije...solar PVs banvayla je material lagta te swatah carbide/nitride and sulphide intensive ahe...plus tyachi life 20-25 years max..so parat 20 years nantr PV change kara...yes it is any day better than coal and thermal but lifecycle carbon footprint still exists, so not completely sustainable...apart from that, a lot of land /surface area is required for these projects...india madhe adhich jamini che vande, tyat navin jamin anaychi kuthun? Plus indian climate is not optimal for solar PV operation, optimal temperature is between 18-24°C where PVs operate at highest efficiency and have longer lifecycles, indian summers where temperatures reach 40-50°C are not ideal for PV operation, after certain years of operations O&M costs would increase exponentially since circuits would melt, short circuits would increase etc, other option is CSV for India where we can actually take advantage of the heat...but that would create heat island effect...we need to rethink our solar strategy"}

{"text":"@Kill Me","mention":{"externalChannelId":"UC1ENkMe8UxvxWE0ge1N1BcQ"}},{"text":" I don't think nationalism is the main issue that hinders our progress, it's more to do with illiteracy within the masses, like India claims 70% literacy but probably only 10% have logical thinking, probably same for Pakistan. 2nd thing is nepotism, it has always been a huge thing in our cultures...the top 1% in politics, business (military for Pakistan) always look for stability for their kin, it doesn't matter to them if their community as a whole would suffer, that's why they are so resistant to change (they don't have 'best for the job' attitude)...if you keep the masses illiterate and impoverished, they would be constants in the ruling class...that is one reason why India chose Modi over Rahul Gandhi...they were desparate for change...and I don't think Congress would come to power until they actually try to solve India's problems. Probably the same reason why Pakistan chose Imran Khan (even though he is rich I don't think he had political connections and stuff, not sure about that, but I guess Pakistans population was tired of Bhuttos and shariffs)...and I can see change in Indian government's attitude towards its problems...they have identified many redundant British era laws that were hampering progress and promoting corruption... Bureaucracy in India has been getting more freedom to implement the right policies, The toilet program is one of that, it is an embarrassing problem but they have recognised it and started to solve it which has improved sanitation facilities; along with health care, micro loans are given to farmers and small shop vendors to start small businesses, affordable housing has helped construct at least 5 crore houses in the last 6 years of Modi gov, highways are being built at 5 times more pace and thier goal is to achieve 8 times more speed by 2024...and the best thing that this gov could have done and which they are doing is increasing transparency...they have made most forms, applications etc online, they have started to use block chain to identify and solve major issues...now I'm not trying to advertise Modi gov, I'm not going to get anything from it, but I can definitely see change, I think what both the populations should understand is that 'you can change your allies but you can't change your neighbours'...so a healthy developed Pakistan would any day be better for both india and pakistan...and if you read all of this, thank you for your patience and hope you have a great day ahead"}

{"text":"@Anant Shinde","mention":{"externalChannelId":"UCyhbawNSGr01A3ElI9lNTJQ"}},{"text":" I did do a study on this during my masters and there are only about three regions in the world that have suitable wind speed and water depth along the coastline, ie norsesea, between England and Scandinavia, northeast usa, and above Taiwan....the thing about offshore wind turbine projects is that minimum turbine design is for 10MW and speeds required for that kind of power generation is 10m/s...so instead of building 200ft+ high turbines in the sea and worry about wind speed and corrosion everyday, 100ft lower capacity turbines (onshore) that require minimum 5m/s for power generation would be more beneficial"}

{"text":"Don't want to be a pessimist, but Solar panel (pv) manufacturing is an energy intensive task plus it takes a huge toll on the environment...plus, the Sun belt is not that suitable for solar pvs since the temperature range is on the higher side and pvs operate better under cooler climatic conditions (between 15-25°C for most efficient generation around 18-20%)... Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) is probably a suitable option since it is based on heat and we can use this tech for maximum solar power in the Sun belt, but again it would be costly and we would be exposing the environment to lot of heavy metals...India for now should invest in material sciences to find a suitable material that can give us solar energy at the highest efficiency, and can be produced locally with minimum environmental impact...pervoskite is one possible option to give efficient generation at high temperatures but again it has expensive heavy metals...but yes, this discussion is needed in India and Indian Colleges should ask their students to invest time in research rather than just obtain a degree"}
